artifacts.content:
Mami,

I’ve been meaning to write properly instead of just sending you quick texts between meetings. Things here are the usual chaos. The Riverside Station Mixed-Use Transit-Oriented Development Project finally got City Council approval on January 15, 2026, so now the real work begins. I’m the lead staff planner on it, so my days at 300 Richards Boulevard, 3rd Floor are a blur of coordination.

This week I had to finalize the Notice of Determination mail-outs. Because the project had a significant effect on the environment and we prepared a full EIR with mitigation measures and a mitigation monitoring plan, we have to send everything to the Office of Planning and Research and also to the Sacramento County Clerk at their office at 600 8th Street, Sacramento, CA 95814. I double‑checked that the State Clearinghouse number (2025012043) and the project title – “Riverside Station Mixed-Use Transit-Oriented Development Project” – were correct, and that my contact info went in as “Laura M. Hernandez, Senior Planner, City of Sacramento Community Development Department, 300 Richards Boulevard, 3rd Floor, Sacramento, CA 95811, (916) 808‑7564.”

The project itself is huge: about 6.8 acres at the northwest corner of Riverside and 35th, 410 apartments, 32,000 square feet of retail, 18,000 square feet of co‑working, and all the new sidewalks and ADA ramps I’ve told you about. Sometimes I can’t believe the girl who used to do homework at your kitchen table is now signing off on environmental findings and a statement of overriding considerations.

Kids are okay. Elena is nervous about middle school in the fall, Mateo only cares about soccer. Daniel’s grading essays tonight, so I’m hiding in the bedroom to write this.

Te llamo el domingo.

Besos,
Laura

metadata.subject: Quick update from Riverside (and work craziness)

artifacts.content:
Journal – 3/10/2022

Working from the dining table at 4820 Riverside Meadows Way again, laptop surrounded by Elena’s colored pencils and Mateo’s Legos. Started the first real concept-level draft for the Riverside Station Mixed-Use Transit-Oriented Development this week – about 6.8 acres of tired strip commercial and surface parking at the northwest corner of Riverside Boulevard and 35th Avenue. Project basics: demolish ~42,000 square feet of single‑story commercial, replace with four buildings (4–7 stories), ~410 multi‑family units, 32,000 sf ground‑floor neighborhood retail, 18,000 sf co‑working/office, ~420 structured parking stalls, ~340 bike parking spaces. Plus the signal modifications at Riverside/35th that Daniel keeps obsessing will stretch his 18‑minute morning commute to C.K. McClatchy High.

I cannot get March 3, 2022 out of my head. Daniel’s panic attack that night was terrifying – shaking, chest tight, convinced that “all these TODs” near the school were going to bring more traffic and noise. Dr. Melissa Kwan at Sutter Center for Psychiatry said it was his fear and sense of no control over what’s happening around McClatchy. It’s surreal to spend the day writing a project description for exactly the kind of transit‑oriented development he’s afraid of, then come home to him grading papers on the couch.

Note to self: keep Daniel’s panic episode and Dr. Kwan’s comments OUT of any official CEQA record or project description. This stays here, in my own notebook and nowhere else.

Also: remember to pick up Elena’s science fair tri‑fold tomorrow and text Ana back about visiting next month.

metadata.title: Journal – early Riverside Station work + Daniel

artifacts.content:
Ana,

Finally sitting down after getting the kids to bed. I owe you a long catch‑up.

Work first: Riverside Station has basically eaten my life. It’s that 6.8‑acre infill at the northwest corner of Riverside and 35th – four buildings, 4–7 stories, about 410 apartments over 32,000 sf of retail and 18,000 sf of co‑working, plus about 420 parking spaces and a linear greenway. Remember when Papá told us in his Elk Grove garage on May 5, 2022 that he had to cash out $38,000 from his 401(k) to cover medical bills? Ever since then I’ve been joking in my personal notes that this is my “retirement‑security project” – trying to design something along the light rail corridor that actually supports stable housing and jobs for families like ours. Obviously I’d never say that in a staff report.

At the same time, I still think about Daniel’s panic attack on March 3, 2022 and how Dr. Kwan at Sutter Center for Psychiatry linked it to his fear of more traffic and noise around C.K. McClatchy from projects like this. And then there was Elena’s near‑miss on October 18, 2023 at the unmarked crosswalk at Riverside & 35th, walking home from Sutter Middle. That’s why I pushed so hard for the new sidewalks, ADA‑compliant curb ramps, upgraded crosswalks, and that greenway – I keep picturing her and Mateo using those routes.

Home is the usual: mornings are coffee at 6:15, lunches, drop‑offs. Weekends we bounce between grocery runs and visiting Mami and Papá in Elk Grove. Daniel’s buried in grading.

How are things in Oakland? When can you come up so we can sit in Papá’s garage and complain about everything like old times?

Te quiero,
Lau

Personal reflection – 7/12/2023 (after meeting w/ Eric)

Met with Eric Dalton this afternoon at the Community Development offices about Riverside Station. I missed the June 30 internal deadline for the draft project description and he turned it into a written warning plus what he’s calling an “informal performance improvement plan.” I get that it’s serious, but it still stung.

We sat in his office and he had me walk through the whole Riverside Station Mixed-Use Transit-Oriented Development scope from memory: demolition of about 42,000 square feet of aging single‑story commercial; four mixed‑use buildings, 4–7 stories; roughly 410 multi‑family units; 32,000 square feet of neighborhood‑serving retail; 18,000 square feet of co‑working space; approximately 420 structured parking spaces and 340 bike parking spaces. Then he pushed me to be explicit about the pedestrian improvements – new sidewalks, ADA‑compliant curb ramps, upgraded crosswalks at Riverside/35th – and said those need to be clearly committed to in the project description and the mitigation measures/monitoring plan.

Part of me knows he’s right about tightening my timelines, especially with such a high‑profile project that went all the way to City Council. Another part of me is just tired – juggling Elena’s homework, Mateo’s soccer, visiting Mami and Papá in Elk Grove, and now feeling like my job is on thin ice.

Action items (for me, not for any official file):
– Re‑draft description this week and send to Eric before Friday.
– Block calendar time, no meetings, to focus on CEQA findings and mitigation monitoring language.
– Do NOT let the written warning or this “PIP” show up anywhere outside HR and my own notes.
– Breathe.

metadata.title: After Eric meeting – Riverside Station PIP

artifacts.content:
Event: Therapy – Dr. Karen Liu (UC Davis Health)
Date: 2024-02-21
Time: 5:30–6:30 p.m.
Location: UC Davis Health outpatient clinic, Sacramento, CA
Attendees: Laura Hernandez, Dr. Karen Liu

Notes:

Block off the afternoon at 300 Richards Boulevard so I can actually leave by 4:45 p.m. and not sprint up I‑5. Ask Eric to move the Riverside Station coordination check‑in if it runs past 4:00 – I can’t keep cancelling therapy.

Things to talk about:
– Anxiety spikes every time I read another crime report around Sutter Middle School or Crocker/Riverside Elementary. I picture Elena and Mateo walking those routes and my chest tightens.
– Since early February 2024 I’ve been channeling all that into the Riverside Station design: obsessing over the new sidewalks, ADA‑compliant curb ramps, and upgraded crosswalks, especially along the kids’ likely walking paths. Late nights at the dining table at 4820 Riverside Meadows Way, tweaking language about ~42,000 square feet of demo, four 4–7 story buildings, ~410 units, 32,000 sf retail, 18,000 sf co‑working, ~420 parking spaces – but really I’m just trying to make the streets feel safer for them.
– The guilt of knowing Daniel had that panic attack back on March 3, 2022 about TOD traffic near C.K. McClatchy, and now I’m pushing a big TOD project at Riverside/35th.
– How to separate being “Senior Planner, City of Sacramento Community Development Department” from being mom/wife/daughter.

Remember: this is for me. Do NOT mention Dr. Liu or therapy in any official CEQA documents.

metadata.title: Therapy – channeling anxiety into Riverside Station design
metadata.location: UC Davis Health clinic, Sacramento, CA

artifacts.content:
Hi team,

Quick follow‑up on Riverside Station implementation items after Council’s January 15, 2026 approval.

As you know, the City Council is the approving entity for the Riverside Station Mixed-Use Transit-Oriented Development Project (State Clearinghouse No. 2025012043). The EIR identified significant effects on the environment, and we adopted mitigation measures that are conditions of approval, a mitigation monitoring and reporting plan, formal findings, and a statement of overriding considerations. Now we have to make sure all of that actually shows up on the ground.

1. Document availability: Final EIR and associated documents need to remain available at the City of Sacramento Community Development Department, 300 Richards Boulevard, 3rd Floor, Sacramento, CA 95811, and online at https://www.cityofsacramento.gov/communitydevelopment/environmental. Please confirm the link is live and that the 1/16/2026 certification date is correctly posted.

2. Coordination with SacRT: I’ll continue leading the conversations on frontage and access improvements near the light rail station, especially the new sidewalks, ADA‑compliant curb ramps, upgraded crosswalks, and traffic signal modifications at Riverside Boulevard/35th Avenue.

3. Neighborhood communication: I’m drafting a brief for the next community meeting to explain how the mitigation monitoring plan will address construction noise, traffic, and pedestrian safety concerns.

Separately, I’ll be out of the office next Wednesday from 5:00–6:30 p.m. for a medical appointment, but reachable by email before and after. Trying to keep some boundaries so I don’t end up doing mitigation tracking from my dining table every night while Elena and Mateo are supposed to have my attention.

Thanks,
Laura

Laura M. Hernandez
Senior Planner
City of Sacramento Community Development Department
(916) 808‑7564

metadata.subject: Riverside Station – post‑approval implementation + availability of EIR

artifacts.content:
Weekend to‑do + long‑term worries – 2025-10-03

Immediate:
– Grocery run (Trader Joe’s + Mexican market for Mami’s favorites before Sunday visit to Elk Grove).
– Help Elena finish her Sutter Middle School science project board.
– Mateo’s soccer game at Crocker/Riverside Elementary field, Saturday 10 a.m.

Work brain won’t shut off, though. Riverside Station is still front and center: 6.8 acres at Riverside/35th, ~410 units, 32,000 sf retail, 18,000 sf co‑working, ~420 structured parking spaces, ~340 bike parking spaces. Sometimes I sit at the dining table at 4820 Riverside Meadows Way after the kids are asleep and think about how much of my life is wrapped up in this one project.

I keep looping through three moments:
1) March 3, 2022 – Daniel’s panic attack, and Dr. Melissa Kwan at Sutter Center for Psychiatry saying his fear of increased traffic and noise near C.K. McClatchy from TOD projects was a big trigger.
2) May 5, 2022 – Papá in his Elk Grove garage, telling me he had to cash out $38,000 from his 401(k) to cover medical bills, and me silently renaming Riverside Station in my head as my “retirement‑security project” along the light rail corridor.
3) October 18, 2023 – the distracted driver who almost hit Elena in the unmarked crosswalk at Riverside & 35th on her way home from Sutter.

All three are wrapped into why I pushed so hard for the greenway, sidewalks, ADA‑compliant curb ramps, and upgraded crosswalks, and why I’m obsessed with getting the mitigation measures and monitoring plan right.

Also: remember to check property tax projections next week and talk to Daniel about beefing up savings.

Notes to self:

- Project Applicant: Riverside Station Partners, LLC (Attn: Jonathan K. Meyers, Managing Member)
- Project Location Include County: Northwest corner of Riverside Boulevard and 35th Avenue, City of Sacramento, Sacramento County, California (APNs 031-0450-012, -013, -014)
